A series of moving vans
efficiently move large amounts of data
Network is the road
the truck is the Internet Protocol
Boxes hold your data
inside the boxes are more things
application information

-Transported inside of IP (encapsulated by the IP Protocol)
-Two ways to move data (different features for different applications)
-Referred to OSI Layer 4 (Transport Layer)
-Multiplexing allows you to use many different applications at the same time

A connection-oriented, guaranteed-delivery
protocol used to send data packets between computers over a network like the Internet. flow control the receiver can manage how much data is sent

A connectionless Transport-layer protocol that is one of the protocols in the Internet protocol suite, and is used with IP. It is also known as the Universal Datagram Protocol. no flow control, sender determines the amount of data transmited
The IP delivery truck delivers from on IP address to another IP address
boxes arrive at the house, where do boxes go? each box has a room name

qas.q: port is written outside of the box
qas.a: drop the box into the right room

• IPv4 sockets - Server IP address, protocol,
server application port number
• Client IP address, protocol, client port number
• Non-ephemeral ports - permanent port numbers
• Ports 0 through 1,023
• Usually on a server or service
• Ephemeral ports - temporary port numbers
• Ports 1,024 through 65,535
• Determined in real-time by the client

• TCP and UDP ports can be any number
between 0 and 65,535
• Most servers (services) use non-ephemeral
(not-temporary) port numbers
• This isn't always the case - it's just a number.
• Port numbers are for communication, not security
• Service port numbers need to be "well known"
• TCP port numbers aren't the same as UDP port numbers

*Web server - tcp/80

*VoIP server - udp/5004

*Email server - tcp/143
